problem_summary: 2018 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy) asked teams to act as a policy-analysis team and design a pricing system for private information (PI), then translate it into government policy recommendations. This O-award paper (Team 83744) reframes the open-ended policy problem as a microeconomics/market-design problem: it treats PI as a leasable commodity and builds a two-stage sequential game between an individual (sell or not) and an organization (steal or not), derives the equilibrium price the government should post, then extends to a network model where PI disclosure has externalities corrected by Pigovian tax/subsidy. Deliverables include a one-page MEMORANDUM to the "President of Government" with seven concrete, parameter-justified recommendations.
models_used: Two-stage dynamic game of perfect information (individual sells/rejects, then organization steals/not-steals) solved by backward induction for the subgame-perfect Nash equilibrium; payoffs encoded in a decision tree (Fig 1)

Government pricing rule derived as the equilibrium-supporting price interval [(1-ps)r1 - w, (1-ps)(b+r2)], with the posted price set to the interval midpoint via a Shapley-value 'equal split of surplus' argument: P = 1/2[(1-ps)b + (1+ps)r2]

Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP, Saaty) to set the weight vector a over three sub-risks (marketing harassment r11, property loss r12, discrimination r13) using 3x3 reciprocal pairwise-comparison matrices for three PI domains (social media A1, financial A2, health A3), eigenvector-derived weights

Risk quantification sub-models: r11 = call-length x wage (labor-economics time-value), r12 = insurance price x wealth-rank theta (revealed-preference via Alipay/ZhongTong fraud insurance), r13 = IS-vector dot V-vector (binary sensitive-characteristic indicators)

Information-amount quantification x = C * ln(n+1), where C = (pieces of basic PI)/15 (completeness, borrowed from a Wikipedia pharmacology-completeness metric) and the log captures diminishing marginal value of profitable PI (microeconomic utility-function analogy); explicitly rejects Shannon entropy as context-blind
Supply-and-demand / comparative-statics model for the no-government competitive market (Fig 2), with curve intercepts tied to the same game parameters
Timeliness (dynamic) sub-models: linear decay x(t)=x0 - lambda*t for basic PI (Poisson/binomial change-count process), exponential decay x(t)=x0*e^(-ln2*t/h) (half-life form) for profitable PI

Generational risk-perception model: subjective risk r1' = mu*r1, with mu = e^(-(p-5.1)/1.1) a bijection from a polynomial-fit willingness-to-take-risk-vs-age curve (4th-degree fit to SOEP survey medians)

BA (Barabasi-Albert) scale-free network of 10,000 nodes as the social graph; agent-based disclosure simulation where node i discloses iff payoff w_i-(1-ps)r_i-d > 0; social utility U includes externality term e*v_i (v_i = node degree)

Pigovian tax/subsidy optimization: grid search over externality e in [-0.2,0.2] and tax d in [-1,1] to find d_m maximizing U; result that negative externality limits government leverage while positive externality (e.g. CDC disease data) warrants subsidy (negative tax)
key_techniques: Reframing an open policy prompt as a solvable equilibrium problem: the whole paper hangs on one clean game whose equilibrium IS the pricing system, so every later question becomes parameter manipulation rather than a new model

Parameter-as-policy-lever design: exogenous parameters (r1, w, b, ps) describe the world; government parameters (P, r2) are the levers, making policy recommendations fall out mechanically as comparative statics
Borrowing legitimacy via named theorems/methods (Shapley value for fair split, AHP for weights, Pigovian tax for externality, BA model for the network) instead of inventing ad hoc machinery
Revealed-preference monetization of intangibles: pricing privacy risk off real insurance premiums (Alipay/ZhongTong 9.9 CNY) and labor time-value rather than guessing
One unifying state variable x (amount of information) that feeds b(x) and w(x), so risk, value, timeliness and breach all reduce to moving x
Reusing the SAME base game across five distinct application sections (human-rights, timeliness, generational, breach, network) — model leverage, not model proliferation
Closing the loop to a deliverable: every modeling result is restated as a concrete bullet in a President-addressed memo
why_it_won:
"From a judge's seat, this paper wins because it answers a vague 'design a pricing system' policy prompt with a single, rigorous, defensible mechanism — a subgame-perfect equilibrium price — and then demonstrates extraordinary leverage by reusing that one model to address every facet of the problem (whether privacy is a human right, time decay, generational difference, mass breaches, network externalities). It speaks the grader's expected language: it correctly deploys Shapley value, AHP, Pigovian taxation, and BA scale-free networks, citing canonical sources for each, which signals mathematical maturity and breadth without being a method dump because each method earns its place. Crucially it nails the COMP requirement of Problem F: it does not stop at math but converts comparative statics into seven specific, actionable, individually-justified policy recommendations in a polished one-page memo to a decision-maker. The economics framing (treating PI as a leasable commodity, internalizing externalities) is novel-feeling yet grounded in real literature (Acquisti, Varian), and the scale-free network externality simulation gives a genuinely fresh extension that the team itself flags as 'quite new in related fields.' The combination of a clean tractable core, demonstrated breadth, real-data parameter grounding, and a crisp decision-ready deliverable is exactly the O-award profile."
"section_highlights": "Strongest sections: (2.5) the game-theory pricing core — a compact decision tree, explicit backward induction, the equilibrium price INTERVAL, and the Shapley midpoint selection; this is the spine everything else hangs on. (2.3) risk modeling — turning a fuzzy 'risk of disclosure' into three measurable sub-risks each anchored to a real proxy (wage x call time, fraud-insurance price, sensitive-trait indicators) and AHP-weighted per domain; concrete and auditable. (4) the network extension — BA scale-free graph + agent-based disclosure simulation + Pigovian d_m grid search delivering the memorable result that government can subsidize positive-externality data (CDC) and tax negative-externality apps. The opening Summary Sheet + MEMORANDUM framing immediately establishes the policy-analyst persona and previews all results. The dual flowcharts (game-theory branch and graph-theory branch) give the reader an early roadmap.",
section_highlights: Strongest sections, judge view: (2.5) the game-theory pricing core is the standout — a clean decision tree (Fig 1), explicit backward induction to the subgame-perfect equilibrium, an equilibrium price INTERVAL rather than a point, and a Shapley-value argument to pick the fair midpoint P = 1/2[(1-ps)b + (1+ps)r2]. This is the spine the whole paper hangs on. (2.3) risk modeling converts a fuzzy 'risk of disclosure' into three measurable sub-risks each anchored to a real-world proxy (wage x call-time; Alipay fraud-insurance price x wealth rank; sensitive-trait indicator vector) and AHP-weights them differently per domain (social media / financial / health) — concrete and auditable. (4) the network extension delivers the memorable, citable result via a BA scale-free graph + agent-based disclosure simulation + Pigovian d_m grid search: government can subsidize positive-externality data (CDC disease records) and tax negative-externality apps, with d_m moving monotonically against e (Figs 7-8). The Summary Sheet + President-addressed MEMORANDUM framing instantly establishes the policy-analyst persona and front-loads all results, and the two roadmap flowcharts (game branch / graph branch) orient the reader before the math.
pitfalls_or_limitations: No sensitivity analysis or robustness check at all — the team admits this; for a paper that introduces ~30 parameters with hand-chosen functional forms, the absence of 'how do conclusions move when r1, b, w, ps shift' is the biggest scientific gap a judge would flag

Functional forms are largely asserted, not validated: x = C*ln(n+1), mu = e^(-(p-5.1)/1.1), the half-life PI_p decay, and the AHP reciprocal matrices are all 'devised by us' with little empirical grounding
Core model is one-individual-vs-one-organization; real PI markets are many-to-one and individuals retain resale ownership (assumption flagged but never modeled), so the equilibrium may not scale

Perfect-information assumption is unrealistic for privacy (parties rarely know true r1, b, ps); the team concedes this but builds nothing for the asymmetric-information case, which is arguably the heart of real privacy economics
Cost of protecting PI (software, time) is omitted from payoffs, which would shift every equilibrium
The breach (3.4) and timeliness (3.2) sections are largely qualitative comparative-statics narratives rather than quantified results — thinner than the game and network sections
Network simulation uses toy parameters (b=0.1, ps=0.05, w_i,r_i ~ U[0,1]) with no calibration, so the dramatic U-vs-e and d_m-vs-e curves are illustrative, not predictive
Some derivations are sloppy in the transcription (sign/notation drift between r and r2, the r11 numeric example), the kind of small algebra inconsistency that judges notice
reusable_patterns: The 'one equilibrium core + N application sections' architecture: build a single tractable model whose solution IS the answer, then address every sub-question by perturbing its parameters — maximum coverage, minimum new machinery. Directly transplantable to any policy/economics MCM-F problem.

Split parameters into descriptive (world) vs control (government/decision-maker) sets, so policy recommendations emerge as comparative statics on the control parameters — turns 'what should the government do' into 'increase r2 until condition X holds'

Monetize intangibles via revealed preference: price an abstract risk/benefit off an existing market price (insurance premiums, wages, ad-revenue figures) and cite it — far more defensible than guessing a constant

Use AHP with small reciprocal matrices to set weights when no data exists, and justify the resulting weights against intuition — a clean, judge-friendly way to handle 'we have no data for these weights'
Pair a named fairness/optimality theorem with your equilibrium (here Shapley value) to justify selecting ONE solution from a feasible set/interval rather than leaving the answer ambiguous

Add a graph-theory/network extension with a BA scale-free graph + simple agent-based disclosure rule to capture externalities/contagion — a reusable 'extension' template that reads as novel and earns breadth points
Apply Pigovian tax/subsidy as the standard policy instrument whenever your model exhibits an externality term — and report the optimal tax as a function of externality magnitude via a parameter sweep

Open with a Summary Sheet + a stakeholder-addressed MEMORANDUM that front-loads every result as a numbered, individually-justified recommendation — the deliverable framing graders reward on policy problems
Include twin roadmap flowcharts at the start (one per major model branch) so the judge can see the whole logical structure before the equations
